htmlContent:
Your body is not broken. It's working.

You're lying in bed. The lights are off. You're finally still after a long day. And then it happens. A muscle in your calf jumps. Or your eyelid flutters. Or your whole leg kicks like you're stepping on a gas pedal in your sleep.

You wonder if something is wrong. You wonder if you're deficient in something. You wonder if this is yet another sign that your body has turned against you.

Here's the thing. It's not. I've been there too. And what I've learned from studying the nervous system is that those twitches and jerks are not your enemy. They are your body trying to finish something it started hours, days, or even years ago.

Stay with me here. Because this changes everything about how you see those nighttime movements.

The freeze response and the unfinished discharge

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching animals in the wild. He noticed something strange. When a gazelle escapes a lion, it doesn't just run away and then go back to grazing. It shakes. Trembles. Twitches. Its muscles contract and release in waves.

Levine called this "discharge." It's the body's way of completing the stress response cycle. The nervous system builds up immense energy to survive a threat. Then it needs to release that energy. Otherwise, the charge stays stuck in the tissues.

Think about it. You've been holding tension all day. Maybe you had a difficult conversation you didn't fully speak your truth in. Maybe you sat at a desk for eight hours with your shoulders up by your ears. Maybe you watched the news and felt helpless. That's energy. That's activation. And it doesn't just disappear because you decide to go to sleep.

Your body knows what to do. It waits until you're safe. Until you're horizontal. Until your conscious mind is offline. And then it starts the cleanup process. Those twitches? That's your nervous system saying, "Okay, we can let go now."

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

Wild, right? Your body is doing exactly what it's supposed to do. It's just that nobody told you that was normal.

Stephen Porges and the vagal brake

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called the "vagal brake." It's a fancy term for how your nervous system regulates your state. When you're safe, your vagus nerve puts a gentle brake on your heart rate and breathing. When you're in danger, it releases that brake so you can fight or run.

Here's where it gets interesting. Many of us live with a chronically released vagal brake. We're stuck in survival mode. And at night, when we finally feel safe enough to drop into rest, the system has to recalibrate. That recalibration can look like twitching, jerking, or even sudden startling sensations just as you're falling asleep.

I've had clients tell me they feel like they're being electrocuted when they try to relax. That's not a sign of damage. That's a sign of a nervous system that has been working overtime for years, maybe decades, and is finally getting permission to slow down.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the nervous system's ability to relax. But I want to be clear: supplements are not a cure. They're support. The real work is understanding what your body is trying to tell you.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your shoulders drop? Did your jaw unclench? That's your system responding to being seen and understood.

The difference between normal discharge and something more

I want to be honest with you. Not all nighttime twitching is benign discharge. There's a difference between a gentle release and a system that's truly stuck.

Normal discharge looks like this: You're drifting off. A muscle in your thigh twitches once or twice. Maybe your foot jerks. It passes. You fall asleep. You wake up feeling okay.

Stuck discharge looks different: You can't fall asleep because your legs won't stop moving. You have a sensation of crawling or electric shocks under your skin. You jerk awake repeatedly, gasping, with your heart racing. You wake up feeling like you never slept at all.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

If you're in that second camp, please hear me. You're not crazy. You're not broken. But your nervous system needs more support than just "waiting it out."

Restless legs syndrome and periodic limb movement disorder are real conditions. They often overlap with trauma histories. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," showed that trauma gets stored in the body at a cellular level. Sometimes that storage shows up as movement disorders.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It helped me understand why my own body wouldn't stop moving at night. It's not a light read. But it's honest.

How to support your body's nighttime discharge

You don't need to stop the twitching. You need to make it safer for your body to do what it's trying to do. Here's what I've found actually helps.

First, create a sense of physical containment. Your nervous system needs to know it's safe to let go. A weighted blanket can provide that deep pressure input that signals safety to your vagus nerve. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). It's like a hug for your entire body. It tells your system, "You're held. You can release."

Second, do conscious discharge before bed. I'm not talking about stretching. I'm talking about intentional shaking. Stand up. Bend your knees slightly. And just shake your whole body for a minute or two. Let your arms flop. Let your legs tremble. It feels ridiculous. It works.

Third, give your body permission to feel. Lie in bed and say to yourself, "It's okay if my legs twitch. It's okay if I jerk. My body knows what it's doing." That might sound silly. But your nervous system is listening. When you stop resisting the discharge, the discharge often completes faster.

Right? Think about it. When you tense against a muscle spasm, it gets worse. When you breathe into it and soften around it, it releases. Same principle applies here.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who works directly with Polyvagal Theory. The book gives you actual exercises, not just theory.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

When the discharge feels like too much

I need to say something that might be uncomfortable. Sometimes the body's nighttime discharge can feel overwhelming. It can trigger flashbacks. It can bring up emotions you weren't ready to feel. That's not a sign that you're doing it wrong. It's a sign that there's a lot stored in there.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who specializes in structural dissociation, talks about how the body holds different parts of our experience. One part might want to release. Another part might be terrified of releasing. That conflict can show up as more twitching, more tension, more struggle at night.

If that's you, please go slow. You don't have to discharge everything at once. You can tell your body, "I know you need to let go. But let's do it in small doses. Let's do it in a way that feels safe."

You can also work with a therapist who understands somatic approaches. Pat Ogden's Sensorimotor Psychotherapy is one modality that directly addresses how the body stores and releases trauma. You don't have to do this alone.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did you feel a small wave of relief? That's your system responding to the possibility of help. That's a good sign.

The truth about the wellness industry and nighttime twitching

I'm going to be direct with you. The wellness industry will tell you that your nighttime twitching is a sign that you're "out of alignment" or that you need to "" or that you need to buy their expensive sleep tea. That's garbage.

Your muscles twitch at night because your nervous system is trying to complete a survival response. That's not a spiritual failing. That's not a character defect. That's biology. That's physiology. That's the legacy of a body that has been doing its best to keep you alive.

Honestly, I get frustrated when I see people being shamed for their bodies' natural processes. You don't need to fix yourself. You need to understand yourself. You need to support yourself. You need to stop fighting the very system that has been working overtime to protect you.

Sit with that. Your body is not your enemy. Your twitching muscles are not a malfunction. They are a message. And once you learn to read that message, the relationship with your body changes.
